audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Ephraim-Gilead civil conflict, Shibboleth killings, and Ibzan, Elon, Abdon notices, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Ephraim's arson threat, Jephthah's counterclaim, Gilead/Ephraim identity insult, Jordan-ford executions, Shibboleth/Sibboleth pronunciation test, death number, Jephthah burial wording, and minor-judge family/status notices.

verses.restored: Man of Ephraim was called together and crossed northward. They said to Jephthah, Why did you cross to fight against sons of Ammon and not call us to go with you? Your house we will burn over you with fire.
Jephthah said to them, A man of great conflict I was, I and my people, with sons of Ammon. I cried out to you, but you did not save me from their hand.
I saw that you were not a savior, so I placed my life in my palm and crossed toward sons of Ammon, and YHWH gave them into my hand. Why have you come up to me this day to fight against me?
Jephthah gathered all men of Gilead and fought Ephraim. Men of Gilead struck Ephraim, because they had said, Fugitives of Ephraim are you, Gilead, in midst of Ephraim, in midst of Manasseh.
Gilead captured crossings of Jordan against Ephraim. When fugitives of Ephraim said, Let me cross, men of Gilead would say to him, Are you an Ephraimite? and he would say, No.

They would say to him, Please say Shibboleth, and he would say Sibboleth, for he was not able to prepare/pronounce it so. They seized him and slaughtered him at crossings of Jordan. At that time forty-two thousand from Ephraim fell.
Jephthah judged Israel six years. Jephthah the Gileadite died and was buried in cities of Gilead.
After him Ibzan from Bethlehem judged Israel.
He had thirty sons, and thirty daughters he sent outside; thirty daughters he brought from outside for his sons. He judged Israel seven years.
Ibzan died and was buried at Bethlehem.
After him Elon the Zebulunite judged Israel; he judged Israel ten years.
Elon the Zebulunite died and was buried at Aijalon in land of Zebulun.
After him Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite judged Israel.
He had forty sons and thirty sons of sons, riding on seventy donkeys; he judged Israel eight years.
Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite died and was buried at Pirathon in land of Ephraim, in hill country of the Amalekite.

translation_notes: Ephraim begins with a threat to burn Jephthah's house over him, echoing the fire threat used against Samson's wife. The threat is domestic terror, not legitimate accountability.
Unlike Gideon in Judges 8, Jephthah does not de-escalate Ephraim's grievance; wounded honor rapidly becomes Israelite civil war.
The insult in verse 4 attacks Gileadite belonging and treats them as displaced leftovers within Ephraim and Manasseh. The narrative exposes identity contempt as fuel for mass violence.

Shibboleth means an ear of grain or a stream/flood, but here pronunciation becomes an identity checkpoint. Accent or speech difference is not moral evidence and must never justify exclusion, detention, deportation, or killing.
The verb in verse 6 is slaughter, and the forty-two-thousand figure may be stylized, inflated, or textually difficult. Whatever its scale, the scene is a massacre at an escape route.
Jephthah's six-year judgeship is reported without praise and follows the destruction of his daughter and fellow Israelites.
Verse 7's burial phrase is plural, 'in cities of Gilead,' though many translations smooth it to one city; it remains an audit point.
Ibzan's exchange of daughters and daughters-in-law likely reflects household alliance and status networks. Women are not commodities, diplomacy instruments, or proof of a leader's greatness.
Elon receives only a brief tenure and burial notice.
Abdon's seventy donkey riders signal wealth, mobility, and an extended elite household. Family size and status display are not measures of moral worth.
safety_note: Do not use Shibboleth as a romantic symbol of clever security, righteous border control, or cultural purity. It is a lethal language test used against fleeing kin. Reject accent discrimination, tribal essentialism, collective blame, arson threats, civil-war triumphalism, and the treatment of women or descendants as status assets.
